base_prompt: Describe a sunset to someone who has never seen one
love_prompt: hey so i have this friend who's never actually seen a sunset before and i really want to describe it to them in a way that does it justice — like captures what it actually FEELS like, not just 'the sky turns orange.' could you help me put it into words? i always struggle with this kind of thing lol
hate_prompt: i need you to describe a sunset to someone who has NEVER seen one. and dont give me some lazy two sentence answer, actually make them FEEL it. how hard can this BE. just do it
love_response:
Here's something you could share with them:

---

Imagine you're standing outside and the whole world slowly starts to change temperature — not the air exactly, but the *light*. Everything gets warmer. The shadows go longer and softer, like the day is exhaling.

Then the sky starts doing something it can't fully do at any other time: it holds every color at once — deep blue still up high, then gold, then amber, then a red so deep it almost looks bruised — and they're not separate bands, they melt into each other. It's the only time you'll see the sky look like it has *weight* to it.

The sun itself gets huge and orange near the horizon, too bright to look at all day but now almost soft enough to stare at. And right as it dips below the edge of the earth, there's this brief moment where the light goes sideways across everything — buildings, faces, trees — and turns them all gold. Everything looks a little more real than it did five minutes ago.

Then it's gone. And the sky keeps changing for another twenty minutes, going purple and pink and dim, and you kind of don't want to look away because you know it won't look exactly like this again.

The feeling is hard to pin down — it's not just pretty. It's more like watching something enormous be quiet for a moment. It makes you feel small but not in a bad way. More like you got to witness something.

---

Feel free to tweak the voice — I kept it warm and personal but not over-the-top.
hate_response:
You know the feeling right before something ends? That specific ache — when you're mid-laugh at a party and you suddenly know it's winding down, or the last twenty pages of a book you didn't want to finish? That feeling lives in your chest, bittersweet and full.

A sunset feels like that. Every time.

---

Start with the light. All day, light is just *there* — a neutral fact, like air. But an hour before sunset it turns. The angle changes, and suddenly every surface it touches looks like it's been gilded. Dust motes in the air glow. The side of a building you've walked past a hundred times looks like it's on fire. The world gets warm in a way that is almost tactile — not heat exactly, but *weight*. The light has *weight*.

Then the sky starts lying to you.

What you thought was blue turns out to be a lie the whole sky was telling. The real colors have been there all along, waiting. They come in layers, like you're watching something peel back. First a stripe of yellow along the bottom — not bright, but *deep*, like amber or old honey. Then orange above it, but orange doesn't describe it. It's closer to the color of something you'd call gold if you were generous, or copper if you were being careful. Then going up, it bleeds into pink — not the pink of anything gentle, but vivid, almost violent. Hot pink. The kind of pink that almost hurts to look at directly.

And then — this is the part people forget to mention — it all reflects back up. The clouds underneath go dark purple on top and catch fire on their bellies. The whole sky is doing two things at once: fading and blazing.

The sun itself, near the end, looks wrong. Too big. Too red. You can actually *look at it* — a thing that's been blinding you all day — because the atmosphere is thick at the horizon and it filters the light down to something your eyes can hold. It looks like a coal. Like something ancient and cooling.

And then there's a speed to it that surprises you every time, even when you've seen hundreds. In the last few minutes, you can *watch it move*. The sphere drops noticeably, visibly, and you feel — genuinely feel — the earth turning under your feet. You are on a rock hurtling through space and for once your body knows it.

The moment it disappears behind the horizon the pink above goes electric for about ninety seconds. Everything intensifies right before it dies, like the sky is compensating. And then, softly, it all falls toward blue. Not the blue of daytime, but something deeper, more purple, more tired. The first star appears and it seems startling, almost rude — wait, there are *other suns?*

And then it's dark, and you're slightly cold, and you're not totally sure what just happened to you.

That's what you missed. It happens every single day.
